planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; golden bamboo REQUIRES a bamboo root barrier (18-inch deep polypropylene sheet) before planting — it is a highly aggressive running bamboo that will invade neighboring properties and is invasive in many southeastern US states without containment.

directions: Plant ONLY within a contained area using an 18-inch deep root barrier in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; golden bamboo (also called fishpole bamboo for its use in making fishing poles) is the most widely planted and most problematic bamboo in the southeastern US — it has naturalized and become invasive along streams, roadsides, and disturbed areas from the Carolinas to Texas, outcompeting native vegetation. Despite its invasive potential, it remains widely sold and planted because of its fast growth, dense screening ability, and striking golden-yellow canes as they mature. The culms turn yellow-gold with age (green when young), and the distinctive swollen internodes near the base of young shoots are characteristic. If used, ALWAYS contain with a root barrier and remove rhizomes that escape under or through the barrier. Harvest young edible shoots when they emerge in spring. Listed as invasive in multiple southeastern states.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in partial shade to full sun; arrow bamboo spreads by rhizomes but less aggressively than Phyllostachys species. A root barrier is still recommended. More shade-tolerant than most bamboos, tolerating deep shade under trees.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in partial shade to shade; arrow bamboo is a Japanese bamboo widely used in Japanese gardens for its excellent shade tolerance — more tolerant of deep shade than virtually any other bamboo. The large, dark green, lance-shaped leaves on each culm branch tip create a lush, tropical appearance. In Japanese tradition, the straight, strong culms were cut and used for arrows (hence the common name 'arrow bamboo'). A spreading bamboo (running rhizomes) but less aggressive than the Phyllostachys species — still benefits from a root barrier to keep contained. Creates dense, weed-suppressing coverage under trees where few other plants grow. More versatile in shade gardens than other bamboos, making it valuable for shady woodland edge screening, Japanese garden composition, and naturalistic planting under large trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; hedge bamboo is a CLUMPING bamboo (Bambusa genus) with tight, non-invasive rhizomes — no root barrier needed. This is one of the most reliable non-invasive bamboos for warm climates, ideal for privacy screens and hedges.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; hedge bamboo (and its many cultivars including the striped 'Alphonse Karr' with yellow culms streaked green) is one of the most widely planted bamboos in the southeastern US, Gulf Coast, and California — a clumping bamboo that does not spread invasively but grows into a graceful, dense clump of arching culms perfect for screens, hedges, and specimen plantings. Because it is Bambusa (a clumping bamboo genus), it does not send out long, invasive rhizomes like the Phyllostachys running bamboos — making it suitable for gardens where invasive spread is a concern. The dense, arching clumps provide excellent year-round visual screening. 'Alphonse Karr' with its yellow and green striped culms is one of the most popular ornamental bamboos in warm climates. Grows vigorously in the Gulf Coast and Florida.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; weeping lovegrass is extremely drought tolerant and is used extensively for erosion control on dry, lean banks, roadsides, and disturbed areas in the Southeast and Southwest. Can be invasive in some areas — check local status.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; weeping lovegrass is a South African perennial grass introduced to the US as an erosion control and forage grass for dry, poor soils, producing attractive, arching, fine-textured foliage and delicate, pendulous flower and seed clusters in summer that give the plant its 'weeping' common name. It has naturalized widely in the Southwest and Southeast, becoming invasive in some dryland habitats including Texas grasslands and southeastern pine flatwoods where it outcompetes native grasses. Despite its invasive risk in some regions, it remains valuable for immediate stabilization of disturbed, dry, eroded slopes where native grasses are slow to establish. Check local invasive status before planting in the Southwest or Southeast — in some states it is listed as invasive. In the driest parts of the Southwest where alternatives are scarce, it provides rapid, long-term erosion control.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky, lean soil in full sun; black grama is a desert grassland native adapted to the hottest, driest conditions of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran desert grasslands and requires excellent drainage and full sun. Extremely drought tolerant once established.

directions: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky, lean soil in full sun; black grama is one of the most important and characteristic native grasses of the Chihuahuan Desert grassland — the dominant grass of desert grasslands from western Texas and southern New Mexico through Arizona into Sonora and Chihuahua. The name 'black grama' comes from the characteristic dark woolly hairs (eriopo = woolly-footed) at the nodes of the stolons. A warm-season grass that spreads by stolons to form a low, sod-like turf in dry desert conditions. Extremely important as a livestock forage grass in the Chihuahuan Desert and a key indicator of healthy desert grassland condition — when overgrazing leads to shrub encroachment (mesquite or creosote) replacing the grass, black grama is typically the first native grass to disappear and one of the last to recover. Highly drought tolerant, essentially requiring no supplemental irrigation in appropriate desert climates. An essential component of Chihuahuan Desert and desert grassland restoration planting mixes.
